title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Bharat Steel Industries Pvt. Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no opportunity of hearing was granted prior to the issuance of the termination order dated 14th March 2022.

The domestic inquiry conducted against the petitioner was vitiated as the Inquiry Officer was an interested party having a direct supervisory relationship with the complainant, rendering the entire inquiry proceedings void ab initio.

The petitioner had completed more than 240 days of continuous service in the preceding twelve months and was therefore entitled to the protection under Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, which was not complied with by the respondent employer.
The charge-sheet issued to the petitioner was vague and did not disclose specific dates, times, or particulars of the alleged misconduct, thereby prejudicing the petitioner in preparing his defence.
The respondent company failed to produce any independent evidence before the Labour Court to substantiate the charges of insubordination and financial irregularity levelled against the petitioner.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee, setting aside the award of the Labour Court which had upheld his termination. The Court held that the domestic inquiry was fundamentally flawed due to bias in the appointment of the Inquiry Officer and non-compliance with Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. The petitioner was directed to be reinstated with fifty percent back wages from the date of termination.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 3847 of 2022

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
versus
BHARAT STEEL INDUSTRIES PVT. LTD. & ANR. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 18th September 2023

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ARVIND PRASAD SINGH

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 07th January 2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. III, Karkardooma, Delhi, whereby the reference made by the appropriate government was decided against the petitioner and his termination from service was upheld.

2. The petitioner was employed as a Senior Accounts Executive with Respondent No. 1 since 12th February 2015. By an order dated 14th March 2022, his services were terminated on the ground of alleged financial irregularities and insubordination. The petitioner raised an industrial dispute, which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, after considering the evidence, dismissed the claim of the petitioner.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Vivek Anand, submitted that the termination order was passed in complete violation of the principles of natural justice. He further contended that the domestic inquiry was vitiated inasmuch as the Inquiry Officer, Mr. P.K. Gupta, was the direct reporting supervisor of the complainant, Mr. Harish Chandra Mehra, thereby creating a reasonable apprehension of bias. Reliance was placed upon the judgment of the Supreme Court in Workmen of Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co. of India v. Management, (1973) 1 SCC 813.

4. It was further argued that the mandatory requirements of Section 25-F of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 were not complied with. The petitioner had admittedly completed more than 240 days of service in the twelve calendar months preceding his termination and was therefore entitled to one month's notice or wages in lieu thereof, and payment of retrenchment compensation. The respondent failed to demonstrate compliance with these statutory requirements.

5. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent company submitted that the petitioner was terminated for proven misconduct and that the inquiry was conducted fairly and in accordance with the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.

6. I have considered the rival submissions and perused the record carefully.

7. On the question of bias in the inquiry proceedings, this Court finds merit in the petitioner's contention. The Inquiry Officer, Mr. P.K. Gupta, held a supervisory position over the complainant-witness. The principle that no man shall be a judge in his own cause is a cornerstone of natural justice. An inquiry officer who shares a hierarchical relationship with the complainant cannot be said to be impartial. The entire inquiry proceedings are therefore liable to be set aside.

8. Furthermore, on the question of Section 25-F compliance, the respondent has placed no material on record to demonstrate that retrenchment compensation was tendered or that the requisite notice was given. This procedural non-compliance itself renders the termination invalid in law.

9. The Labour Court fell into error in not appreciating these fundamental infirmities in the termination proceedings and in mechanically accepting the respondent's case.

10. In view of the foregoing, the impugned award dated 07th January 2022 is hereby set aside. The petitioner shall be reinstated to his original post within four weeks from the date of this judgment. He shall be entitled to fifty percent of back wages from the date of termination till the date of actual reinstatement.

11. The writ petition is accordingly allowed with costs of Rs. 25,000 to be paid by Respondent No. 1 to the petitioner.

(ARVIND PRASAD SINGH)
JUDGE
SEPTEMBER 18, 2023
